transcript:
Right now you're watching two thousand pale dots drift and wobble in loose orbit — nervous, uncertain, each doing its own small thing. Then without warning it snaps: everything rushes to center, the screen flashes red and shakes, and a white "I" burns into place. Then it settles into a gold afterglow before dissolving back into that same nervous drift, over and over.

Here's what got me: those two thousand points aren't decoration — they're literally called "voters" in the code, each one pre-assigned a "vote X" and "vote Y" position before the animation even starts. During deliberation each one gets its own hue offset, a tiny individual "opinion" in color. Then in the snap phase, that function calculating the collapse uses a deliberately brutal cubic ease — the artist's comment literally says "not gentle easing, VIOLENT compression." And the "I" doesn't appear until phaseProgress passes 0.5 — it only gets to exist once the collapse is already half-finished.

So the letter you read as "self" is the last thing rendered, and the only thing that was never uncertain.

color. Then in the snap phase, that function calculating the collapse
